title: Gundam Creator Yoshiyuki Tominos thoughts on Eva
display_title: Gundam Creator Yoshiyuki Tominos thoughts on Eva
wikitext:
[]: Gundam Creator Yoshiyuki Tomino's thoughts on Eva             Quote Post by Holy Diver » Thu Jun 17, 2010 2:43 pm So I was very upset when I saw Evangelion, because it was apparent to me that the people who made it weren't thinking at all about making fun for or gaining the sympathy of the audience. Instead they tried to convince the audience to admit that everybody is sick, practically in the middle of a nervous breakdown, all the time. I don't think you should show things like that to everybody. It's not entertainment for the masses--it's much more interested in admitting that we're all depressed nervous wrecks, I thought. It was a work that told people it was okay to be depressed, and it accepted the psychological state that said if you don't like the way the world works, then it's okay to just pick up a gun and attack someone. I don't think that's a real work of art. When people see that, they begin to realize they are the same way. I think that we should try to show people how to live healthier, fuller lives, to foster their identity as a part of their community, and to encourage them to work happily until they die. I can't accept any work that doesn't say that.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Thu Jun 17, 2010 3:12 pm yeah, lolTomino related link is relevant, particularly the 8th post http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=2878&postdays=0&postorder=asc&start=0 I've heard that Brain Powerd sucked, though. He claims it's not "his answer/his antithesis" to NGE, but comparisons between the two are supposedly inescapable. I wouldn't know since I haven't seen it, though it IS on my round tuit list.

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Thu Jun 17, 2010 3:31 pm I think it's ironic that Kill 'Em All Tomino says something like that about Evangelion.

[]:             Quote Post by carla » Thu Jun 17, 2010 3:32 pm tomino-san: YES. and now i shall go and have myself an EVA marathon. lol.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Thu Jun 17, 2010 3:55 pm Wow. Tomino would fit right in with some of the regulars here.

[]:             Quote Post by Holy Diver » Thu Jun 17, 2010 6:18 pm The irony is even greater when you consider the fact that Tomino once said that all of the deaths in his own works (Zeta and Victory Gundam come to mind) are a form of catharsis that calms his homicidal urges.

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Thu Jun 17, 2010 6:31 pm It is truly bizarre to hear this sort of thing from somebody who makes a living creating war stories. The emotional and psychological damage caused by war cripples just as well as anything out there on the battlefield. And that's just the adults in modern standing armies. Now imagine how a child is going to deal with all that. The "art" this guy is talking about seems more like willfully naive fantasy. Also, I don't think Evangelion ever tells us to "give in" and accept things for what they are. Didn't the story come from, in part, Anno-san's own battle to escape depression? This reminds me of the people who hate that Shinji whines. Most adolescents would not fare nearly as well as he does. It is quite admirable that he doesn't shut down completely until the final episodes. Hell, witnessing a car crash is enough to give some people PTSD, let alone witnessing and partaking in the apocalypse. Just getting through the first episode would be enough to put people in the mental health ward for months. I'm not that familiar with Tomino's body of work, but how many of his characters are adolescents or children? Isn't it more irresponsible to portray such characters as anything other than damaged? Being a child soldier isn't awesome.

[]:             Quote Post by Holy Diver » Thu Jun 17, 2010 6:49 pm oOoOoOo wrote: I'm not that familiar with Tomino's body of work, but how many of his characters are adolescents or children? Isn't it more irresponsible to portray such characters as anything other than damaged? Being a child soldier isn't awesome.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jun 17, 2010 7:18 pm Funny to see what I transcribed ages ago from Animerica , Vol 8 #2 (March 2000) made its way from EGF to /m/ back to EGF again. Original source including more of the interview with Eva bits: http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?p=71487#71487 AND THE CIRCLE IS COMPLETE!

[]:             Quote Post by child of Lilith » Thu Jun 17, 2010 7:24 pm Sounds like sour apples to me.

[]:             Quote Post by Clover » Thu Jun 17, 2010 7:30 pm "I think that we should try to show people how to live healthier, fuller lives, to foster their identity as a part of their community" If this man watched every part of the series, then he is completely missing the point when he says that Evangelion does the opposite of this.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Thu Jun 17, 2010 8:20 pm @SSD : yeah, I already linked to that. oOoOoOo wrote: I'm not that familiar with Tomino's body of work, but how many of his characters are adolescents or children? Isn't it more irresponsible to portray such characters as anything other than damaged? Being a child soldier isn't awesome.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jun 17, 2010 8:29 pm I think I said all I had to say about this in THAT thread.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Jun 18, 2010 1:02 am Merridian wrote: both of those featured mentally unhinged and depressed kids piloting the eponymous giant robots.

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Fri Jun 18, 2010 6:59 am I've actually lost a bit of respect for Tomino due to the hypocrisy and narrow-minded definition of art expressed in that quote; not to mention that he seemed to misinterpret the show to the point of gleaning the exact opposite meaning from it, which says a lot about his capacities as a viewer/reader.

[]:             Quote Post by Halicat » Fri Jun 25, 2010 4:18 pm I was just going to start watching Mobile Suit Gundam as well, I am a major fan of Mobile Suit Gundam Wing so I wanted to see what it was based on, however, after reading that quote I have the feeling I'm going to come out pretty confused about the intention, since this will be my first viewing of any of Tomino's works

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Fri Jun 25, 2010 4:40 pm I think Tomino is one of the most overrated anime makers, to be honest. I've never seen anything by him that I thought was more than decent. I haven't seen Ideon, to be fair, but every Gundam I've ever seen has left me saying, "meh".

[]:             Quote Post by berto » Fri Jun 25, 2010 5:40 pm There is a bit of Japanese traditionalist mentality to his words. While Eva could be costruded as a piece on the woes of the individual the traditionalist phylosophy emphesises the needs of needs of the community and puts a negative spin on the individual, and sometimes on people of independant tought, a sort of 'put your needs aside and focus on the community.' Don't get me wrong, in it of itself it's not a bad philosophy within reason, like all other philosophies. It's a school of thought that allows people to be part of something bigger than themselves, and, again, within reason that's not a bad thing. Anyway, to say that he refuses to accept anything that doesn't follow his criteria as art is a rather narrow minded mentality, I don't apretiate it nor do I agree with it, and to tell lies about it is rather unbecoming, and it was a lie because there was no instance where someone took a gun just because they didn't like the way the world was. You know I'm getting the distinct impression that he is saying that people shouldn't meke shows that show people been depressed or angry, you know, like in real life? Because that will make people realize that they feel that way, too. Rather, people should make shows about happy people that are only happy because they are part of a community and anthing that doens't follow that criteria isn't art. I'll be honest, I don't know where this guy studied art history but what his describing isn't art, it's propaganda.

[]:             Quote Post by Holy Diver » Fri Jun 25, 2010 6:39 pm Halicat wrote: I was just going to start watching Mobile Suit Gundam as well, I am a major fan of Mobile Suit Gundam Wing so I wanted to see what it was based on, however, after reading that quote I have the feeling I'm going to come out pretty confused about the intention, since this will be my first viewing of any of Tomino's works

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Fri Jun 25, 2010 10:42 pm ZapX wrote: I think Tomino is one of the most overrated anime makers, to be honest. I've never seen anything by him that I thought was more than decent. I haven't seen Ideon, to be fair, but every Gundam I've ever seen has left me saying, "meh".

I don't think you should show things like that to everybody. It's not entertainment for the masses--it's much more interested in admitting that we're all depressed nervous wrecks, I thought. It was a work that told people it was okay to be depressed, and it accepted the psychological state that said if you don't like the way the world works, then it's okay to just pick up a gun and attack someone. I don't think that's a real work of art. When people see that, they begin to realize they are the same way. I think that we should try to show people how to live healthier, fuller lives, to foster their identity as a part of their community, and to encourage them to work happily until they die. I can't accept any work that doesn't say that.
